title_en: Introduction to Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir and Legal Maxims
title_id: Pengantar Al-Asybah wan-Naza'ir dan Kaidah-Kaidah Fikih
content_id:
Al-Asybah wan-Naza'ir (Kemiripan dan Persamaan) karya Zayn ad-Din Ibn Nujaym al-Mishri (wafat 970 H / 1563 M) adalah koleksi definitif kaidah-kaidah fikih dan prinsip-prinsip mazhab Hanafi. Judul tersebut merujuk pada dua genre literatur hukum Islam yang terkait namun berbeda: al-asybah (kemiripan) merujuk pada kasus-kasus yang serupa satu sama lain dalam ciri-ciri lahirnya namun berbeda dalam hukumnya, sementara al-naza'ir (persamaan) merujuk pada kasus-kasus yang benar-benar sejajar dan memiliki hukum yang sama. Karya ini menyatukan keduanya, mengorganisasikan kumpulan besar hukum kasus Hanafi di bawah prinsip-prinsip umum yang memungkinkan pelajar dan ulama memperluas hukum ke situasi-situasi baru.

Ibn Nujaym lahir di Kairo dan menerima pendidikan hukumnya di sana, menjadi salah satu ulama Hanafi terkemuka pada periode Utsmaniyah. Beliau menulis secara produktif tentang fikih Hanafi dan sangat terkenal atas syarah besarnya Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq, suatu eksposisi terperinci mazhab Hanafi berdasarkan Kanz ad-Daqa'iq. Al-Asybah wan-Naza'ir, bagaimanapun, adalah karyanya yang paling banyak dibaca dan berpengaruh, yang diadopsi sebagai rujukan standar dalam pendidikan hukum Hanafi di seluruh Kesultanan Utsmaniyah, Asia Selatan, dan Mesir.

Genre kaidah-kaidah fikih (al-qawa'id al-fiqhiyyah) muncul dari pengakuan bahwa kumpulan besar hukum kasus Islam dapat diorganisasikan di bawah prinsip-prinsip umum yang diturunkan dari Al-Quran, Sunnah, dan konsensus yang telah mapan dari para ulama. Kaidah-kaidah ini bukan sumber hukum tersendiri — otoritasnya diturunkan dari hukum-hukum yang diringkasnya — tetapi berfungsi sebagai alat yang kuat untuk penalaran analogis, memungkinkan fuqaha menerapkan prinsip-prinsip yang telah mapan pada situasi-situasi baru.

Ibn Nujaym mencontoh karyanya secara substansial pada Al-Asybah wan-Naza'ir Syafi'i karya Taj ad-Din as-Subki (wafat 771 H), mengadaptasi struktur dan banyak kaidah ke posisi-posisi dan contoh-contoh mazhab Hanafi sendiri. Beliau juga mengacu pada kompilasi Hanafi awal oleh Ibn as-Sa'ati dan karya-karya lain dalam genre tersebut. Pembahasannya jauh lebih sistematis dibandingkan upaya-upaya Hanafi sebelumnya dalam alur yang sama.

Karya ini dibuka dengan lima kaidah fikih fondasi (al-qawa'id al-kulliyyah) yang diterima di seluruh empat mazhab Sunni: (1) segala urusan dinilai berdasarkan tujuannya (al-umuru bi-maqasidiha); (2) keyakinan tidak hilang dengan keraguan (al-yaqin la yazul bisy-syakk); (3) kesulitan mendatangkan kemudahan (al-masyaqqah tajlib at-taysir); (4) bahaya harus dihilangkan (adh-dharar yuzal); dan (5) adat/kebiasaan itu dapat menjadi hukum (al-'adah muhakkamah). Masing-masing dari kelima kaidah ini mendapat pembahasan luas dalam Al-Asybah wan-Naza'ir, dengan puluhan kaidah cabang dan ratusan kasus ilustratif yang diturunkan dari masing-masing kaidah.

Karya ini kemudian melanjutkan untuk mencakup kaidah-kaidah fikih yang tersisa yang khusus untuk fikih Hanafi, diikuti oleh pembahasan tentang kaidah-kaidah yang tersebar, aplikasi analogis baru, dan perbedaan-perbedaan penting antara otoritas-otoritas terkemuka mazhab (Imam Abu Hanifah, Abu Yusuf, dan Muhammad asy-Syaybani). Karya ini ditutup dengan bagian-bagian tentang metodologi penalaran hukum dan prinsip-prinsip yang mengatur dalil-dalil yang saling bertentangan.

Bagi pelajar hukum Islam, Al-Asybah wan-Naza'ir karya Ibn Nujaym — bersama rekannya dari mazhab Syafi'i karya as-Suyuti — merepresentasikan gerbang penting menuju logika struktural fikih dan sebuah mahakarya tentang bagaimana prinsip-prinsip menyatukan berbagai hukum-hukum parsial yang sebaliknya membingungkan.
content_en:
Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir (Resemblances and Analogues) by Zayn ad-Din Ibn Nujaym al-Masri (d. 970 AH / 1563 CE) is the definitive collection of legal maxims and principles of the Hanafi school. The title refers to two related but distinct genres of Islamic legal literature: al-ashbah (resemblances) refers to cases that resemble one another in their apparent features but differ in their legal ruling, while al-naza'ir (analogues) refers to cases that are genuinely parallel and share the same ruling. The work brings together both, organizing the vast body of Hanafi case law under general principles that allow students and scholars to extend the law to novel situations.

Ibn Nujaym was born in Cairo and received his legal education there, becoming one of the foremost Hanafi scholars of the Ottoman period. He wrote prolifically on Hanafi jurisprudence and is particularly celebrated for his major commentary Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq, a detailed exposition of the Hanafi school based on Kanz ad-Daqa'iq. Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir, however, is his most widely read and influential work, adopted as a standard reference in Hanafi legal education across the Ottoman Empire, South Asia, and Egypt.

The genre of legal maxims (al-qawa'id al-fiqhiyyah) arose from a recognition that the vast body of Islamic case law could be organized under general principles derived from the Quran, the Sunnah, and the established consensus of the scholars. These maxims are not themselves legislative sources — they derive their authority from the legal rulings they summarize — but they serve as powerful tools for analogical reasoning, allowing jurists to apply established principles to new situations.

Ibn Nujaym modeled his work substantially on the Shafi'i Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir of Taj ad-Din as-Subki (d. 771 AH), adapting the structure and many of the maxims to the Hanafi school's own positions and examples. He also drew on the earlier Hanafi compilation by Ibn as-Saati and other works in the genre. His treatment is considerably more systematic than earlier Hanafi efforts in the same vein.

The work opens with the five foundational legal maxims (al-qawa'id al-kulliyyah) that are accepted across all four Sunni legal schools: (1) matters are judged by their objectives (al-umuru bi-maqasidiha); (2) certainty is not removed by doubt (al-yaqin la yazul bish-shakk); (3) hardship brings ease (al-mashaqqah tajlib at-taysir); (4) harm must be removed (ad-darar yuzal); and (5) custom is authoritative (al-'adah muhakkamah). Each of these five maxims receives extensive treatment in Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir, with dozens of subsidiary maxims and hundreds of illustrative cases derived from each.

The work then proceeds to cover the remaining legal maxims particular to Hanafi jurisprudence, followed by a discussion of scattered maxims, novel analogical applications, and important differences between the school's leading authorities (Imam Abu Hanifah, Abu Yusuf, and Muhammad ash-Shaybani). It concludes with sections on the methodology of legal reasoning and the principles governing conflicting evidences.

For students of Islamic law, Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir by Ibn Nujaym — alongside its Shafi'i counterpart by as-Suyuti — represents an essential gateway into the structural logic of fiqh and a masterclass in how principles unify an otherwise bewildering array of particular rulings.
content_ar: Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir (Resemblances and Analogues) by Zayn ad-Din Ibn Nujaym al-Masri (d. 970 AH / 1563 CE) is the definitive collection of legal maxims and principles of the الحنفي school. The title refers to two related but distinct genres of Islamic legal literature: al-ashbah (resemblances) refers to cases that resemble بعضهم بعضاً in their apparent features but differ in their legal ruling, بينما al-naza'ir (analogues) refers to cases that are genuinely parallel and share the same ruling. The work brings together both, organizing the vast الجسد of الحنفي case law under general principles that allow students and العلماء to extend the law to novel situations. Ibn Nujaym was born in Cairo and received his legal education there, becoming one of the foremost الحنفي العلماء of the Ottoman period. كتب prolifically on الحنفي jurisprudence and is particularly celebrated for his major commentary Al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq, a detailed exposition of the الحنفي school based on Kanz ad-Daqa'iq. Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir, غير أن, is his most widely read and influential work, adopted as a standard reference in الحنفي legal education across the Ottoman Empire, South Asia, and Egypt. The genre of legal maxims (al-qawa'id al-fiqhiyyah) arose from a recognition that the vast الجسد of Islamic case law could be organized under general principles derived from the القرآن الكريم, the السنة, and the established consensus of the العلماء. These maxims are not themselves legislative sources — they derive their authority from the legal rulings they summarize — but they serve as powerful tools for analogical reasoning, allowing jurists to apply established principles to new situations. Ibn Nujaym modeled his work substantially on the الشافعي Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir of Taj ad-Din as-Subki (d. 771 AH), adapting the structure and many of the maxims to the الحنفي school's own positions and examples. He أيضاً drew on the earlier الحنفي compilation by Ibn as-Saati and other works in the genre. His treatment is considerably more systematic than earlier الحنفي efforts in the same vein. The work opens with the five foundational legal maxims (al-qawa'id al-kulliyyah) that are accepted across all four Sunni legal schools: (1) matters are judged by their objectives (al-umuru bi-maqasidiha); (2) certainty is not removed by doubt (al-yaqin la yazul bish-shakk); (3) الشدة brings اليسر (al-mashaqqah tajlib at-taysir); (4) harm must be removed (ad-darar yuzal); and (5) custom is المعتمد (al-'adah muhakkamah). Each of these five maxims receives extensive treatment in Al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir, with dozens of subsidiary maxims and hundreds of illustrative cases derived from each. The work then proceeds to cover the remaining legal maxims particular to الحنفي jurisprudence, followed by a discussion of scattered maxims, novel analogical applications, and المهم differences between the school's leading authorities (الإمام Abu Hanifah, Abu يوسف ﷺ, and محمد ﷺ ash-Shayb
